module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: A 58-year-old 90kg man was admitted with a 1-week history of fatigue, anorexia, back pain, constipation and abdominal pain. His past medical history included hypertension. Blood results showed an acute rise in serum creatinine to 2.5 times his baseline and a high total calcium level (3 mmol/L). He passed 4L of urine per day. His fractional excretion of sodium was 4%. The urine dipstick showed trace proteinuria. There were no red cell casts, white cell casts or eosinophil. The most likely pathophysiology is:
options: a. Acute obstruction from kidney stones
questions.explanation: Myeloma is the most likely cause. The patient presents with back pain, constipation, abdominal pain, polyuria and hypercalcaemia — classic manifestations of myeloma. High FENa (4%) indicates intrinsic renal disease. There was no renal ultrasound to confirm obstruction from kidney stones. Clinical presentations were not in keeping with sepsis. Trace proteinuria could occur in glomerulonephritis and interstitial nephritis, but absence of haematuria and leukocyturia makes both incorrect.
questions.topic: AKI myeloma hypercalcaemia differential diagnosis FENa
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: A 58-year-old 90kg man was admitted with a 2-day history of pyrexia, urinary incontinence and abdominal pain. His past medical history included hypertension, kidney stones and a recent course of ciprofloxacin for urinary tract infection. Blood results showed an acute rise in serum creatinine to 2.5 times his baseline. His fractional excretion of sodium was 4% and coarse granular casts on urine microscopy. The urine dipstick did not show haematuria, leukocyturia or proteinuria. There were no red cell casts, white cell casts or eosinophil. The most likely pathophysiology is:
questions.explanation: Sepsis-induced AKI is the most likely cause. The patient presents with fever, urinary tract symptoms and abdominal pain consistent with sepsis. He showed no signs of dehydration, making hypovolaemia unlikely. There was no history of urinary retention or renal colic to suggest obstruction. Urine dipstick did not show haematuria, leukocyturia or proteinuria, making glomerulonephritis and interstitial nephritis unlikely. High FENa (4%) is consistent with intrinsic renal injury from sepsis.
questions.topic: AKI sepsis differential diagnosis FENa granular casts
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: What does NOT contribute to misdiagnosis of acute kidney injury in critically ill patients?
options: d. Receipt of sulfamethoxazole prior to ICU admission
questions.explanation: Receipt of sulfamethoxazole does NOT contribute to misdiagnosis of AKI. It is trimethoprim (not sulfamethoxazole) that can interfere with tubular secretion of creatinine, causing a rise in serum creatinine without a true fall in GFR. Physiological post-operative changes, obesity (affects creatinine generation and volume of distribution), liver cirrhosis (low creatinine production due to reduced muscle mass and impaired hepatic metabolism), and fluid overload (dilutes creatinine) can all contribute to misdiagnosis by masking true AKI or mimicking it.
questions.topic: AKI misdiagnosis creatinine trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole fluid overload
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: A 25-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU following high-energy polytrauma (cyclist vs car). She undergoes surgical fixation of pelvic and crural fractures with four-compartment fasciotomy. Six hours post-ICU admission she is haemodynamically stable and mechanically ventilated, but has minimal urine output of 5 ml/hr of concentrated urine via urinary catheter placed intraoperatively. Which is the MOST important initial cause to exclude?
options: d. Drug-related AKI from intraoperative antibiotics
questions.explanation: Urethra and bladder injury must be excluded first as it represents a mechanical/obstructive cause that would make fluid resuscitation and standard AKI management futile or harmful. In pelvic trauma, urethral and bladder injuries are common (open-book pelvic fracture is a classic association). A correctly functioning urinary catheter must be confirmed before attributing oliguria to hypovolaemia or AKI. Hypovolaemia is also likely but responds to fluid challenge; rhabdomyolysis, drug-related AKI and sepsis are less immediately urgent to exclude before ensuring catheter patency and urological integrity.
questions.topic: AKI trauma oliguria bladder injury pelvic fracture catheter
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: A polytrauma patient is on ICU day 18 following two laparotomies for abdominal injuries. She develops new oliguria (5–10 ml/hr) and the nurse notices a discharge from the abdominal wound. Urine dipstick shows pH 6, 10 erythrocytes/µL, no leucocytes. Urine creatinine is 3.1 mmol/L. Creatinine measured in fluid from the abdominal drain is 1939 µmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: a. Acute tubular necrosis from sepsis
b. Urinary fistula with intra-abdominal urine leak
c. Acute interstitial nephritis from antibiotics
questions.explanation: The markedly elevated creatinine in the abdominal drain fluid (1939 µmol/L vs serum ~normal) is diagnostic of urinary leak into the peritoneal/abdominal cavity — urine creatinine is far higher than plasma creatinine, so any fluid with very high creatinine concentration indicates urine. This was confirmed by retrograde cystogram showing bladder perforation at the cranial aspect, most likely secondary to the abscess drainage procedure. This case illustrates that creatinine measurement in drain fluid is a rapid, cheap and highly specific test for urinary fistula.
questions.topic: urinary fistula bladder perforation drain creatinine AKI diagnosis
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up

questions.question: A polytrauma ICU patient on day 18 develops new oliguria and abdominal wound discharge. You suspect a urinary fistula. Which single investigation most rapidly confirms intra-abdominal urine leak?
options: d. Creatinine level in fluid from the abdominal drain
questions.explanation: Creatinine measurement in drain fluid is the fastest and most practical first step to confirm urinary leak: a creatinine concentration in drain fluid far exceeding serum creatinine (e.g. >1000 µmol/L) is diagnostic of urine. Retrograde cystogram subsequently localises the perforation site and is the definitive anatomical test, but requires radiology resources. Eosinophil count tests for drug-induced interstitial nephritis. Urine dipstick and urinalysis are useful but non-specific. Renal biopsy is invasive and not indicated acutely here.
questions.topic: urinary fistula diagnosis drain creatinine retrograde cystogram
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part IV: Diagnostic Work-up
